assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is NOT true of this painting?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/923bad83bebcbd3918c8d2265b977424.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Leonardo does not include any of the traditional symbols of the divine in this painting, such as halos**

choices.content: Christ subtly reaches for the bread and wine, anticipating the first Eucharist or communion.
Judas holds his bag of silver, which he was paid to betray Christ.
At the Last Supper, Christ informs the apostles that one of them will betray him. The apostles each react in a very different way.
Leonardo includes all of the traditional symbols of the divine in this painting.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Prior to this painting, most representations of the Last Supper look very similar. What key feature did Leonardo change in his version?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/923bad83bebcbd3918c8d2265b977424.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Leonardo placed Judas on the same side of the table with the other apostles. Traditionally he sits on the opposite side. **

choices.content: Leonardo placed Judas on the same side of the table with the other apostles. Traditionally he sits on the opposite side.
Leonardo did not include any cups of wine. Traditionally there were always cups of wine for the Eucharist.
Leonardo included Mary Magdalene, who was not normally present in paintings of the Last Supper.
Leonardo shows the Last Supper happening in a plain-looking room. Traditionally the room was highly decorated.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Leonardo unified the composition by grouping the apostles together in [[☃ dropdown 1]] groups of [[☃ dropdown 2]] figures.**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/923bad83bebcbd3918c8d2265b977424.jpg)
hints: **Leonardo unified the composition by grouping the apostles together in four groups of three figures each.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Leonardo's *Last Supper* is the altarpiece for the main church space in Santa Maria delle Grazie.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **This painting is in the refectory, which was where the monks of the monastery would have eaten their meals in silence.**
